subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods — Memory, Motivation, and Personality
stimulus:
Professor Gonzalez hypothesizes that students will remember more information from his detailed course
description if it is printed on yellow paper than if it is printed on standard white paper. To test this hypothesis,
he put the names of all his students into a bowl, then drew out names to assign them to two different groups. He
gave one group of the students in his class a course description printed on white paper, and gave the other
group of students a course description printed on yellow paper. Students were instructed to thoroughly read the
description outside of class in preparation for their next class. In the next class, he gave all students a
ten-question quiz asking them about the information found on the course description. Professor Gonzalez then
compared the number of correct answers for each group of students. The statistically significant results are
depicted in the graph.

parts.prompt: State the operational definition of the dependent variable.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that the dependent variable is operationally defined as the  student's score on the course description quiz.

required_elements: The dependent variable is operationally defined as the score on the course description quiz.
alternatives.correct_example: The dependent variable is operationally defined as the score on the course description quiz.

required_elements: The dependent variable is the quiz score/grade on the quiz.
alternatives.correct_example: The dependent variable is the quiz score/grade on the quiz.

required_elements: The number of correct answers on the quiz is the dependent variable.
alternatives.correct_example: The number of correct answers on the quiz is the dependent variable.

wrong_examples: The dependent variable is the type of paper used.
The student's result on the quiz, and that's the dependent variable.
The mean score on the quiz.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that the dependent variable is operationally defined as the student's score on the course description quiz.

sample_responses.response: The dependent variable is operationally defined as the score on the course description quiz.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — directly names the quiz score as the operational definition.

sample_responses.response: The dependent variable is the quiz score/grade on the quiz.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — quiz score/grade is a valid operational measurement.

sample_responses.response: The number of correct answers on the quiz is the dependent variable.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — count of correct answers is an operational measurement.

sample_responses.response: The dependent variable is the type of paper used.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — paper type is the IV, not the DV.

sample_responses.response: The student's result on the quiz, and that's the dependent variable.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — vague 'result' phrasing is not a specific operational definition.

sample_responses.response: The mean score on the quiz.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — the mean is a summary statistic, not the operational definition of the DV itself.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that the experimental group is the group with the course  description printed on yellow paper.

required_elements: The experimental group is the people who have the yellow paper.
alternatives.correct_example: The experimental group is the people who have the yellow paper.

wrong_examples: The experimental group is the yellow paper. Responses that state a control group instead of an experimental group.
Example: The control group is the group with the yellow paper.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that the experimental group is the group with the course description printed on yellow paper.

sample_responses.response: The experimental group is the people who have the yellow paper.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — identifies the people with the treatment (yellow paper).

sample_responses.response: The experimental group is the yellow paper.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — identifies the material, not a group of people.

sample_responses.response: The control group is the group with the yellow paper.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — calls out control group instead of experimental group.

parts.prompt: In addition to the manipulation of an independent variable, identify the procedure Professor Gonzalez used to make this study a true experiment.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that the procedure used to make this a true experiment is random  assignment.

required_elements: Professor Gonzalez randomly assigned the participants to groups.
alternatives.correct_example: Professor Gonzalez randomly assigned the participants to groups.

required_elements: Professor Gonzalez put people in groups randomly.
alternatives.correct_example: Professor Gonzalez put people in groups randomly.

required_elements: Professor Gonzalez puts the names of all students into a bowl and pulls them out of a bowl.
alternatives.correct_example: Professor Gonzalez puts the names of all students into a bowl and pulls them out of a bowl.

wrong_examples: Professor Gonzalez used random selection to make this a true experiment.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that the procedure used to make this a true experiment is random assignment.

sample_responses.response: Professor Gonzalez randomly assigned the participants to groups.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — directly names random assignment.

sample_responses.response: Professor Gonzalez put people in groups randomly.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — paraphrase of random assignment is acceptable.

sample_responses.response: Professor Gonzalez puts the names of all students into a bowl and pulls them out of a bowl.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — describes the specific random-assignment procedure from the stimulus.

sample_responses.response: Professor Gonzalez used random selection to make this a true experiment.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — confuses random selection (sampling) with random assignment.

parts.prompt: Explain how the data as presented in the graph do or do not support Professor Gonzalez's hypothesis.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that data support the hypothesis because the experimental group  (i.e., the yellow paper group) scored higher on the test.

required_elements: Professor Gonzalez thought that the experimental group would score higher, and they did, so the data support the hypothesis.
alternatives.correct_example: Professor Gonzalez thought that the experimental group would score higher, and they did, so the data support the hypothesis.

required_elements: The bar for the yellow paper group is higher than the bar for the white paper group, which supports the hypothesis.
alternatives.correct_example: The bar for the yellow paper group is higher than the bar for the white paper group, which supports the hypothesis.

required_elements: The control group/white paper group scored lower than the experimental group/yellow paper group, which is what Professor Gonzalez expected.
alternatives.correct_example: The control group/white paper group scored lower than the experimental group/yellow paper group, which is what Professor Gonzalez expected.

wrong_examples: The data do not support the hypothesis.
The data support the hypothesis (needs to make clear what the hypothesis was).
scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that data support the hypothesis because the experimental group (i.e., the yellow paper group) scored higher on the test.

sample_responses.response: Professor Gonzalez thought that the experimental group would score higher, and they did, so the data support the hypothesis.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — states support and directional result.

sample_responses.response: The bar for the yellow paper group is higher than the bar for the white paper group, which supports the hypothesis.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — reads the graph and names the directional difference.

sample_responses.response: The control group/white paper group scored lower than the experimental group/yellow paper group, which is what Professor Gonzalez expected.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — states support and directional difference from the control-group perspective.

sample_responses.response: The data do not support the hypothesis.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — contradicts the graph, which shows the yellow group scored higher.

sample_responses.response: The data support the hypothesis.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — incomplete; needs to explain why/what the hypothesis was.

parts.prompt: Explain how Context-dependent memory might relate to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that the student's memory retrieval is affected if some external  element (e.g., room, sensory cues, equipment) that is present when the information was  learned/encoded is present/the same or absent/not the same as when they take a quiz.

required_elements: If students learn the information in a particular classroom, they should take a quiz in that same classroom so they can use the surroundings to cue them for their memories.
alternatives.correct_example: If students learn the information in a particular classroom, they should take a quiz in that same classroom so they can use the surroundings to cue them for their memories.

required_elements: If a student chews gum while studying, they should chew gum while taking a quiz. If students sit in different seats taking the quiz than when they learned that information, they will do worse on the quiz.
alternatives.correct_example: If a student chews gum while studying, they should chew gum while taking a quiz. If students sit in different seats taking the quiz than when they learned that information, they will do worse on the quiz.

wrong_examples: Students must consider context in order to remember things well enough for their quiz.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that the student's memory retrieval is affected if some external element (e.g., room, sensory cues, equipment) that is present when the information was learned/encoded is present/the same or absent/not the same as when they take a quiz.

sample_responses.response: If students learn the information in a particular classroom, they should take a quiz in that same classroom so they can use the surroundings to cue them for their memories.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — matching classroom environment at encoding and retrieval improves memory.

sample_responses.response: If a student chews gum while studying, they should chew gum while taking a quiz.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — sensory cue (gum) matched at encoding and retrieval.

sample_responses.response: If students sit in different seats taking the quiz than when they learned that information, they will do worse on the quiz.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — mismatched external context (different seats) hinders retrieval.

sample_responses.response: Students must consider context in order to remember things well enough for their quiz.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — vague; does not reference matching external cues.

sample_responses.response: If a student is happy while learning the information, they will do better if they are happy when they take the quiz.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — describes mood-dependent or state-dependent memory, not context-dependent.

parts.prompt: Explain how Yerkes-Dodson law might relate to the scenario.

scoring_points.description:
Response must indicate that students would perform best under a level of optimal  arousal. 

OR 

Response must indicate that students would perform worse if they were under- or over- aroused. 

Synonyms for "under-aroused" can include a reference to the amount (e.g., too little,  under, insufficient). 

Synonyms for "over-aroused" can include a reference to the amount (e.g., too much, overly, 

super).

required_elements: Students might be too aroused on the quiz, so they don't do well.
alternatives.correct_example: Students might be too aroused on the quiz, so they don't do well.

required_elements: Students may be at their optimal arousal, so they end up doing well on the quiz.
alternatives.correct_example: Students may be at their optimal arousal, so they end up doing well on the quiz.

required_elements: Students are extremely disinterested/very bored in the class, so they don't do well on a quiz.
alternatives.correct_example: Students are extremely disinterested/very bored in the class, so they don't do well on a quiz.

required_elements: Students who have a high level of arousal perform poorly on the quiz.
alternatives.correct_example: Students who have a high level of arousal perform poorly on the quiz.

wrong_examples: Students may be relaxed, so they do well on the quiz.
Students who are excited do poorly on the quiz.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that students would perform best under a level of optimal arousal. OR Response must indicate that students would perform worse if they were under- or over-aroused. Synonyms for "under-aroused" can include a reference to the amount (e.g., too little, under, insufficient). Synonyms for "over-aroused" can include a reference to the amount (e.g., too much, overly, super).

sample_responses.response: Students might be too aroused on the quiz, so they don't do well.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — over-arousal (with quantity marker) hinders performance.

sample_responses.response: Students may be at their optimal arousal, so they end up doing well on the quiz.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — optimal arousal yields best performance.

sample_responses.response: Students are extremely disinterested/very bored in the class, so they don't do well on a quiz.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — under-arousal (extreme boredom) hurts performance.

sample_responses.response: Students who have a high level of arousal perform poorly on the quiz.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — high arousal reduces performance.

sample_responses.response: Students may be relaxed, so they do well on the quiz.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — 'relaxed' does not reference arousal level relative to optimal.

sample_responses.response: Students who are excited do poorly on the quiz.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — 'excited' without quantity marker is ambiguous.

parts.prompt: Explain how Low level of the Big Five trait of conscientiousness might relate to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: Response must indicate that a low level of an enduring characteristic of conscientiousness would hinder student performance on the quiz (e.g., not being diligent, being less hardworking, being unorganized, being undisciplined, being irresponsible, being unprepared, etc.).

required_elements: Students who aren't hardworking may be hindered from doing well on the quiz.
alternatives.correct_example: Students who aren't hardworking may be hindered from doing well on the quiz.

wrong_examples: Students who are diligent do better on the quiz.
Students who did not study hard did poorly on the quiz.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Response must indicate that a low level of an enduring characteristic of conscientiousness would hinder student performance on the quiz (e.g., not being diligent, being less hardworking, being unorganized, being undisciplined, being irresponsible, being unprepared, etc.).

sample_responses.response: Students who aren't hardworking may be hindered from doing well on the quiz.
sample_responses.commentary: Acceptable per CB — low conscientiousness descriptor (not hardworking) linked to impaired performance.

sample_responses.response: Students who are diligent do better on the quiz.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — describes high conscientiousness, not low.

sample_responses.response: Students who did not study hard did poorly on the quiz.
sample_responses.commentary: Unacceptable per CB — describes a behavior, not the enduring personality trait.
